name: Simple harmonic oscillator (Hooke's law)
conventions: x is displacement from equilibrium; positive x corresponds to compression or extension by convention
k > 0 is the spring (stiffness) constant in N/m
m > 0 is the inertial mass in kg
no damping (ideal oscillator); friction adds a -gamma x_dot term for damped SHO
no external driving force; periodic forcing converts this to a driven harmonic oscillator
small-amplitude approximation: real systems deviate from Hooke's law at large displacements
expectedLimits: t = 0: x = A cos(phi), v = -A omega sin(phi) — set by initial conditions
natural angular frequency: omega = sqrt(k/m); period T = 2 pi sqrt(m/k); independent of amplitude
total mechanical energy: E = (1/2) k A^2 = (1/2) m v_max^2, conserved exactly (closed system)
k -> 0: omega -> 0, free particle (no restoring force)
amplitude A grows unbounded under resonant driving (no damping); damping caps the steady-state amplitude
references: Hooke (1678) — De Potentia Restitutiva — original 'ut tensio sic vis'
Halliday, Resnick & Walker — Fundamentals of Physics, ch.15
Goldstein, Poole & Safko — Classical Mechanics, ch.6
